title: Alex Thompson: Mercedes’ F2 Hopeful Finds Redemption in GT3 — But Not With the Silver Arrows
meta: Published: 12 March 2026 | By Tunde Adebayo, Feature & Human‑Interest Writer | F1CC News
caption: Alex Thompson celebrates an improbable double victory at Auto Club Speedway.
body:
Alex Thompson arrived at the F2 season opener in Melbourne as a Mercedes‑backed prospect, carrying the weight of the three‑pointed star on his shoulders. He left Australia with a solitary point‑less 16th place in the Feature Race, a result that mirrored his practice form: consistently buried in the midfield, never threatening the front. In the cockpit after the chequered flag, he removed his gloves slowly — the left first, then the right — and stared at the dead steering wheel. The silence in the Trident garage was heavier than any reprimand.

But just days later, halfway around the world, Thompson climbed out of a very different car — an Aston Martin Vantage GT3, bare of any Mercedes branding — and did something remarkable. He won both races at Auto Club Speedway’s high‑banked oval. He stood on the top step twice, the green car still ticking hot in the California dusk, his hands shaking not from fear but from the sheer improbability of it. A few days earlier he had been invisible. Now he was a winner, twice over, and the contrast was so sharp it felt like two different lives.

It was a Jekyll‑and‑Hyde performance that has become the defining story of Thompson’s chaotic season.

How the GT3 Spring Challenge Works

The GT3 Spring Challenge is a privateer GT3 championship that runs on alternate weekends from Formula 2. Each round features two races: a 30‑minute Sprint Race with no mandatory pit stops, and a 2‑hour Feature Race requiring strategy, tyre management, and at least one pit stop. Points are awarded deep — 150 for 1st, 130 for 2nd, 115 for 3rd, 105 for 4th, 100 for 5th, 95 for 6th, 90 for 7th, 85 for 8th, 80 for 9th, 75 for 10th, then decreasing by 5 points per position down to 25 for 20th. Positions 21st and beyond score zero.

The grid is a true GT3 melting pot: McLarens, Porsches, Audis, BMWs, Lamborghinis, and Mercedes‑AMG GT3s all compete. Thompson’s Aston Martin Vantage is one of the few without a direct factory presence in the series. The calendar mixes iconic road courses with two oval rounds: Auto Club Speedway (the only true high‑banked oval) and the upcoming Jacarepaguá oval in Brazil. Rounds like Indianapolis and Goiânia use their road course configurations, not the oval layouts.

Two Worlds, One Driver

Thompson’s primary employer, Mercedes‑AMG, has a strong presence in the GT3 Spring Challenge — yet Thompson signed with an independent Aston Martin squad, racing without the factory support he enjoys in F2. When he arrived at Fontana, the green car was waiting for him in a quiet corner of the paddock, no silver arrows on the nose, no factory engineers in the garage. Just a seat that didn’t quite fit his frame and pedals set for a stranger.

“The Mercedes deal is for the single‑seater programme,” Thompson explained after his Auto Club double victory. He was still in his race suit, the sweat drying on his collar. “Here, I’m just a guy in a green car trying to prove I can win anywhere. The fact that it’s an Aston — and that there’s no silver arrows on the nose — doesn’t bother me one bit. A win is a win.” He paused, then added more quietly: “I needed this. I needed to remember I can do it.”

And what a win it was. On the 2‑mile D‑shaped oval of Auto Club Speedway — the only true oval on the entire calendar — Thompson led every lap of the 30‑minute sprint race, then repeated the feat in the two‑hour endurance feature. Two wins, two podiums, and a sudden leap to 37th in the GT3 standings — but with only 602 points, his season remains a rollercoaster.

A Season of Extremes

Thompson’s GT3 campaign has been brutal outside of California:

RoundTrackSprint ResultFeature Result
1Sebring International Raceway28thCrash (DNF)
2Long Beach Street Circuit25th40th
3Auto Club Speedway Oval1st (150 pts)1st (150 pts)
4Indianapolis Motor Speedway Road Course18th (35 pts)19th (30 pts)
5Canadian Tire Motorsport Park (Mosport)34thDNF
6Autódromo Internacional Ayrton Senna (Goiânia)32nd36th

Note: Indianapolis and Goiânia use the road course configurations. The next oval event will be at Jacarepaguá later in the season.

Thompson’s 602 total points come exclusively from his two wins (300 pts) and the 65 points earned at Indianapolis (18th + 19th). Every other round has yielded a finish outside the top 20. His podium tally remains exactly two — both victories. The zeros stretch across the spreadsheet like a wound, and yet the wins are so vivid they almost blot out the rest.

“The oval was a freak result,” admits the Aston Martin team principal, speaking on condition of anonymity due to Thompson’s Mercedes ties. “He’s fast, but he’s also a magnet for trouble. Sebring he binned it in the feature. Long Beach got swallowed by the pack — though the sprint was actually one of his cleaner drives. Mosport the sprint was a disaster and the feature ended with a broken upright. It’s not for lack of pace.” The principal’s voice carried the weariness of a man who has watched a driver throw away good weekends with small mistakes, but also the flicker of someone who knows exactly how rare it is to lead every lap of an endurance race on an oval. “You don’t lead every lap twice and call it luck.”

Thompson himself was candid about his struggles at Goiânia. He spoke while leaning against the Aston Martin’s rear wing, the car still dusty from the Brazilian track. “It is a bit gutting, the result I got. I know there is more pace to the car, somewhere, but it’s knowing where to look. Just gotta keep pushing forward with my head down.” The words were calm, but his jaw was tight. He looked away for a moment, then back. “I’ve been here before — the days when you finish 32nd and wonder if anyone’s even noticing. You get up the next morning anyway.”

F2 Struggles: A Familiar Pattern

In Melbourne, Thompson’s weekend followed a similar script — minus the victory. Driving for Trident, he was anonymous in practice, never higher than 14th, the car’s rear end snapping unpredictably under braking. He squeezed through Q1 only to be eliminated in Q2, his fastest lap leaving him four tenths adrift of the cut. In the Feature Race, he ran as high as 12th before a late spin at Turn 9 dropped him to 16th, scoring no points in either the Sprint or Feature. The spin was not dramatic — a half‑turn on cold tyres, a moment of impatience — but it was the kind of error that separates points from nothing.

“The car was there, but I wasn’t,” Thompson admitted after the race. He was standing in the paddock, the noise of Albert Park fading into the evening. “Jaromir Grunt showed what AIX can do with a perfect lap. We’re missing something.” That “something” might be confidence — the unshakeable belief that when the car wiggles under braking, it will bite again. Grunt, his former F3 rival, took pole and victory in Melbourne, while Thompson watched from the lower half of the top 20. The two had been equals once. Now Grunt is the name on everyone’s lips, and Thompson is the one who finished 16th.

The Bigger Picture

Mercedes‑AMG Formula 2 programme director, when reached for comment, downplayed concerns with the measured tone of a programme that does not discard its investments lightly. “Alex is a long‑term project. One difficult weekend in Melbourne does not define a season. His GT3 outings are supplementary — he has our full support, even if we’re not on the Aston’s bodywork.”

But paddock whispers suggest otherwise. Mercedes is known for demanding focus, and Thompson’s split commitment — combined with his erratic results — has raised eyebrows. His two GT3 wins, however, may be his strongest bargaining chip. He knows this. He carries it.

“No one in F2 has won a race this year,” Thompson pointed out, a flicker of steel breaking through the self‑doubt. “Grunt won the Feature, but that’s one. I’ve won two races in the past month — just in a different series. That counts for something. And I did it in a car that doesn’t even have Mercedes power.” He said it quietly, not as a boast, but as a reminder to himself as much as anyone else.

Next Up: Curvelo Long, March 15th

Thompson will trade his Trident F2 car for the Aston Martin again on March 15th at Autódromo Internacional de Curvelo in Brazil. The 2.6‑mile “Long” layout demands precision over power — a stark contrast to the oval where he triumphed. Curvelo is another road course, like most of the calendar, and will test whether Thompson can finally find consistency away from ovals. The last time he was in Brazil, at Goiânia, he finished 32nd and 36th. He remembers the dust on the car, the long silence in the garage. He does not want to feel that again.

Can he replicate his Auto Club magic? Or will the real Alex Thompson — the one who crashed at Sebring and trundled home 40th at Long Beach — show up again? The question hangs over him like a half‑finished sentence.

“I’ve got two wins, two podiums, and a lot of zeros,” Thompson said, packing his bags for Brazil. The garage was quiet around him, the green Aston Martin already loaded into the transporter. “The zeros hurt. But the wins? They tell me I belong. Now I just need to stop crashing.” He zipped the bag, and the sound was louder in the empty space than any engine roar. For a Mercedes‑backed driver in an Aston Martin, surrounded by a grid full of Silver Arrows competitors, that’s the only sponsorship that matters: belief.

— Additional reporting by GT3 Spring Challenge media.
